qtype: Simplifying ratios, dividing quantities in ratio, percentage reduction
tex:
(a) A plane has 14 First Class seats, 70 Premium seats and 168 Economy seats.
\newline

Find the ratio First Class seats : Premium seats : Economy seats.
\newline

Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

 :  : 

(b) For a morning flight, the costs of tickets are in the ratio

First Class : Premium : Economy = 14 : 6 : 5.
\newline

The cost of a Premium ticket is \$114.
\newline

Calculate the cost of a First Class ticket and the cost of an Economy ticket.
\newline

First Class \$ 
Economy \$ 

(c) For an afternoon flight, the cost of a Premium ticket is reduced from \$114 to \$96.90.
\newline

Calculate the percentage reduction in the cost of a ticket.
\newline

 \%

(d) When the local time in Athens is 09 00, the local time in Berlin is 08 00.
\newline

A plane leaves Athens at 13 15.
\newline

It arrives in Berlin at 15 05 local time.
\newline

Find the flight time from Athens to Berlin.
\newline

 h  min

(e) The distance the plane flies from Athens to Berlin is 1802 km.
\newline

Calculate the average speed of the plane.
\newline

Give your answer in kilometres per hour.
\newline

 km/h

content.content:
A plane has 14 First Class seats, 70 Premium seats and 168 Economy seats.
\newline

Find the ratio First Class seats : Premium seats : Economy seats.
\newline

Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

For a morning flight, the costs of tickets are in the ratio

First Class : Premium : Economy = 14 : 6 : 5.
\newline

The cost of a Premium ticket is \$114.
\newline

Calculate the cost of a First Class ticket and the cost of an Economy ticket.
\newline

First Class \$ \AnswerLine[]
Economy \$ \AnswerLine[]

For an afternoon flight, the cost of a Premium ticket is reduced from \$114 to \$96.90.
\newline

Calculate the percentage reduction in the cost of a ticket.
\newline

When the local time in Athens is 09 00, the local time in Berlin is 08 00.
\newline

A plane leaves Athens at 13 15.
\newline

It arrives in Berlin at 15 05 local time.
\newline

Find the flight time from Athens to Berlin.
\newline

The distance the plane flies from Athens to Berlin is 1802 km.
\newline

Calculate the average speed of the plane.
\newline

Give your answer in kilometres per hour.
\newline

_rawBody:
\begin{parts}
\item A plane has 14 First Class seats, 70 Premium seats and 168 Economy seats.

Find the ratio First Class seats : Premium seats : Economy seats.

Give your answer in its simplest form.

\AnswerLine[] : \AnswerLine[] : \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item For a morning flight, the costs of tickets are in the ratio

First Class : Premium : Economy = 14 : 6 : 5.

The cost of a Premium ticket is \$114.

Calculate the cost of a First Class ticket and the cost of an Economy ticket.

First Class \$ \AnswerLine[]
Economy \$ \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}

\item For an afternoon flight, the cost of a Premium ticket is reduced from \$114 to \$96.90.

Calculate the percentage reduction in the cost of a ticket.

\AnswerLine[] \%
\Marks{2}

\item When the local time in Athens is 09 00, the local time in Berlin is 08 00.

A plane leaves Athens at 13 15.

It arrives in Berlin at 15 05 local time.

Find the flight time from Athens to Berlin.

\AnswerLine[] h \AnswerLine[] min
\Marks{1}

\item The distance the plane flies from Athens to Berlin is 1802 km.

Calculate the average speed of the plane.

Give your answer in kilometres per hour.

\AnswerLine[] km/h
\Marks{2}
\end{parts}

tex:
The box-and-whisker plots show the times spent exercising in one week by a group of women and a group of men.
\newline

Below are two statements comparing these times.
\newline

For each one, write down whether you agree or disagree, giving a reason for your answer.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
Statement & Agree or disagree & Reason \\
\hline
On average, the women spent less time exercising than the men.
\newline
 & & \\
\hline
The times for the women show less variation than the times for the men.
\newline
 & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(a) The frequency table shows the times, t minutes, each of 100 children spent exercising in one week.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline
Time (t minutes) & Frequency \\
\hline
$0 \leq t < 60$ & 41 \\
\hline
$60 \leq t < 100$ & 24 \\
\hline
$100 \leq t < 160$ & 23 \\
\hline
$160 \leq t < 220$ & 8 \\
\hline
$220 \leq t < 320$ & 4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Calculate an estimate of the mean time.
\newline

 min

(b) The information in the frequency table is shown in this cumulative frequency diagram.
\newline

Use the cumulative frequency diagram to find an estimate of

the 60th percentile,

 min

(c) the number of children who spent more than 3 hours exercising.
\newline

(d) A histogram is drawn to show the information in the frequency table.
\newline

The height of the bar for the interval $60 \leq t < 100$ is 10.8 cm.
\newline

Calculate the height of the bar for the interval $160 \leq t < 220$.
\newline

 cm

_rawBody:
\relinput{Figures/Q03-BoxWhiskerPlots.tex}

The box-and-whisker plots show the times spent exercising in one week by a group of women and a group of men.

Below are two statements comparing these times.

For each one, write down whether you agree or disagree, giving a reason for your answer.

\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
Statement & Agree or disagree & Reason \\
\hline
On average, the women spent less time exercising than the men. & & \\
\hline
The times for the women show less variation than the times for the men. & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\Marks{2}

\begin{parts}
\item The frequency table shows the times, t minutes, each of 100 children spent exercising in one week.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline
Time (t minutes) & Frequency \\
\hline
$0 \leq t < 60$ & 41 \\
\hline
$60 \leq t < 100$ & 24 \\
\hline
$100 \leq t < 160$ & 23 \\
\hline
$160 \leq t < 220$ & 8 \\
\hline
$220 \leq t < 320$ & 4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

Calculate an estimate of the mean time.

\AnswerLine[] min
\Marks{4}

\item The information in the frequency table is shown in this cumulative frequency diagram.

\relinput{Figures/Q03b-CumulativeFrequency.tex}

Use the cumulative frequency diagram to find an estimate of

the 60th percentile,

\AnswerLine[] min
\Marks{1}

\item the number of children who spent more than 3 hours exercising.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item A histogram is drawn to show the information in the frequency table.

The height of the bar for the interval $60 \leq t < 100$ is 10.8 cm.

Calculate the height of the bar for the interval $160 \leq t < 220$.

\AnswerLine[] cm
\Marks{2}
\end{parts}

Number of white dots & 1 & 4 & 9 & 16 \\
\hline
Number of black dots & 0 & 1 & 3 & 6 \\
\hline
Total number of dots & 1 & 5 & 12 & 22 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\hline
Diagram & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
Number of white dots & & \\
\hline
Number of black dots & & \\
\hline
Total number of dots & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

[2]

\newline

(c) The expression for the total number of dots in Diagram n is

$\dfrac{1}{2}(3n^2 - n)$.
\newline

Find the total number of dots in Diagram 8.
\newline

(d) Find an expression for the number of black dots in Diagram n.
\newline

Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

(e) T is the total number of dots used to make all of the first n diagrams.
\newline

T = an$^3$ + bn$^2$

Find the value of a and the value of b.
\newline

You must show all your working.
\newline

a = 
b =

_rawBody:
\begin{parts}
\item Factorise completely.

$3a^2b - ab^2$

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item Solve the inequality.

$3x + 12 \leq 5x - 3$

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item Simplify.

$\sqrt[3]{x^2 y^4}$

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item Solve.

$\dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{x}{2 - x}$

x = \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}

\item Expand and simplify.

$(x - 2)(x + 5)(2x - 1)$

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}

\item Alan invests \$200 at a rate of r\% per year compound interest.

After 2 years the value of his investment is \$206.46.

Show that $r^2 + 200r - 323 = 0$.

[3]

\item Solve the equation $r^2 + 200r - 323 = 0$ to find the rate of interest.

Show all your working and give your answer correct to 2 decimal places.

r = \AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}
\end{parts}

tex:
There are 32 students in a class.
\newline

5 do not study any languages.
\newline

15 study German (G).
\newline

18 study Spanish (S).
\newline

(a) Complete the Venn diagram to show this information.
\newline

[2]

(b) A student is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that the student studies Spanish but not German.
\newline

(c) A student who studies German is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this student also studies Spanish.
\newline

(d) A bag contains 54 red marbles and some blue marbles.
\newline

36\% of the marbles in the bag are red.
\newline

Find the number of blue marbles in the bag.
\newline

(e) Another bag contains 15 red beads and 10 yellow beads.
\newline

Ariana picks a bead at random, records its colour and replaces it in the bag.
\newline

She then picks another bead at random.
\newline

Find the probability that she picks two red beads.
\newline

(f) Find the probability that she does not pick two red beads.
\newline

(g) A box contains 15 red pencils, 8 yellow pencils and 2 green pencils.
\newline

Two pencils are picked at random without replacement.
\newline

Find the probability that at least one pencil is red.
\newline

stem.content:
There are 32 students in a class.
\newline

5 do not study any languages.
\newline

15 study German (G).
\newline

18 study Spanish (S).
\newline

_rawBody:
There are 32 students in a class.

5 do not study any languages.

15 study German (G).

18 study Spanish (S).

\begin{parts}
\item Complete the Venn diagram to show this information.

\relinput{Figures/Q09-VennDiagram.tex}

[2]

\item A student is chosen at random.

Find the probability that the student studies Spanish but not German.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{1}

\item A student who studies German is chosen at random.

Find the probability that this student also studies Spanish.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{1}

\item A bag contains 54 red marbles and some blue marbles.

36\% of the marbles in the bag are red.

Find the number of blue marbles in the bag.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item Another bag contains 15 red beads and 10 yellow beads.

Ariana picks a bead at random, records its colour and replaces it in the bag.

She then picks another bead at random.

Find the probability that she picks two red beads.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{2}

\item Find the probability that she does not pick two red beads.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{1}

\item A box contains 15 red pencils, 8 yellow pencils and 2 green pencils.

Two pencils are picked at random without replacement.

Find the probability that at least one pencil is red.

\AnswerLine[]
\Marks{3}
\end{parts}
